description: Explains how marimo derives a notebook dependency graph from cell definitions and references, how that graph drives reactive execution, stale and disabled behavior, and dataflow visualization.
document:
marimo's reactive runtime is built around the idea that a notebook is a dataflow program, not a top-to-bottom transcript. The user-facing docs describe each cell as ordinary Python that marimo statically analyzes to find the global variables it defines and the global variables it reads. From those relationships, marimo builds a directed graph on cells. When a cell runs, every cell that references a global variable defined by that cell is affected: in the normal eager mode it is scheduled to run, while lazy or expensive-notebook configurations can mark affected cells stale instead. This is why page order does not control execution order, why cells can be organized out of narrative order, and why deleting a cell also removes its globals from program memory.

The central implementation coordinator is `DirectedGraph`, which owns the graph state and delegates specialized work to smaller dataflow components. Its topology stores cells plus parent and child links, where an edge means the child reads something produced by the parent. A definition registry records which cell defines each name and also carries the extra information needed for SQL definitions and hierarchical SQL references. Edge computation combines those two views: a newly registered cell becomes a child of cells that define its references, and existing cells that refer to its definitions become children of it. Python definitions can flow into SQL cells, but SQL definitions are not treated as Python references. Cells that delete variables are also wired into the graph so that deletion can invalidate cells depending on the deleted name.

The graph is maintained incrementally as cells are registered and deleted. Registration first records the cell's definitions, then computes parent and child edges, and then asks the cycle tracker whether any added edge closes a path back to its source. Deletion unregisters the cell's definitions, removes graph edges and topology entries, and drops cycles that depended on removed edges. The implementation uses a lock around mutating graph operations because the same graph can be shared by the kernel and code-completion service. Graph helpers expose ancestors, descendants, paths, multiply-defined names, stale cells, and deleted nonlocal references without requiring callers to know the internal storage layout.

The notebook runner turns a requested set of root cells into an execution queue. It always includes stale ancestors of the requested roots, because those upstream values are needed before a root can run. In autorun mode it also includes descendants, using graph traversal and import-block pruning, and then topologically sorts the selected cells so parents run before children. Disabled cells are skipped directly, and descendants of disabled cells are treated as transitively disabled until the blocking cell is re-enabled. If a running cell raises an exception or is interrupted, the runner cancels affected descendants in the current queue so they do not execute against invalid upstream state.

The runtime's graph model also explains marimo's editor dataflow tools. The variables explorer shows each variable's name, value, defining cell, and use sites from the same static understanding of definitions and references. The dependencies panel exposes a minimap for the current cell's local context and a graph view for the whole notebook. The minimap reads left to right: direct inputs are cells the current cell reads from, direct outputs are cells that read from it, and nearby indirect cells are transitive dependencies. Because a marimo cell can define multiple variables, downstream tracing is intentionally broader than upstream tracing: if a dependent cell references any definition from the selected cell, the dependent cell is considered affected.
